variables.v_sep: Relative speed of separation after collision (m/s)
variables.v_app: Relative speed of approach before collision (m/s)
variables.h_drop: Height from which ball is dropped (m)

locked_facts: e = (relative speed of separation) / (relative speed of approach) = |v2' - v1'| / |u1 - u2|
e = 1: perfectly elastic collision (no KE loss)
e = 0: perfectly inelastic collision (objects stick, no separation)
For ball dropped from height h: e = √(h_bounce / h_drop)
KE retained after collision: KE_final/KE_initial = e² (for ball bouncing off fixed surface)
After n bounces: h_n = e^(2n) × h_0
truth_statements: The coefficient of restitution is a measure of the elasticity of a collision, ranging from 0 to 1
For a perfectly elastic collision, the relative speed of separation equals the relative speed of approach (e = 1)
In a perfectly inelastic collision, the bodies do not separate after impact, so e = 0
The value of e depends on the materials of the colliding bodies and their shapes, not on their masses
A ball dropped from height h bounces to height e²h, demonstrating progressive energy loss each bounce

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three balls dropped from same height: steel ball (e ≈ 0.95) bounces almost to original height. Rubber ball (e ≈ 0.8) bounces to ~64% height. Clay ball (e ≈ 0) sticks to ground. Height bars show h_bounce = e²h_drop for each.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Side-by-side bounces. Steel: nearly returns. Rubber: noticeable loss. Clay: dead stop. e² values and bounce heights displayed.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that e is a material property that quantifies elasticity — rubber is NOT the most elastic for collisions

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A ball held at height h above a hard surface. Drop height h marked. e value displayed. Formula e = √(h_bounce/h_drop) shown. KE bar at zero (ball at rest).
simulation_states.emphasis: Introduce the setup — ball about to be dropped. What height will it bounce to?

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Ball falls, hits surface, bounces. Impact flash at contact. Bounce height h_bounce measured and compared to h_drop. Ratio displayed: h_bounce/h_drop = e². KE before and after impact shown — KE_after = e² × KE_before.
simulation_states.emphasis: Energy lost during impact: fraction e² retained, fraction (1 - e²) lost to deformation and heat

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Three materials compared: steel (e = 0.95), rubber (e = 0.8), clay (e = 0.05). All dropped from same height. Steel bounces highest, clay barely bounces. After 5 bounces: steel still bouncing, rubber much lower, clay stopped at bounce 1.
simulation_states.emphasis: e is a material property — higher e means more elastic collision. Each bounce loses (1 - e²) fraction of energy.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust e value (0 to 1) and drop height. Watch multiple bounces. Counter shows bounce number, height, and KE retained. Try e = 1 (ball returns to exact height forever) and e = 0 (ball sticks on first impact).
simulation_states.emphasis: Slide e from 0 to 1 and see bounce behavior change dramatically. Count how many bounces until height < 1 cm.

common_misconceptions.belief: Rubber balls are the most elastic because rubber is 'elastic'
common_misconceptions.correction: In physics, 'elastic collision' means KE is conserved, not that the material stretches. Steel-on-steel collisions (e ≈ 0.95) are MORE elastic than rubber-on-rubber (e ≈ 0.8) because steel deforms less and loses less energy to internal friction.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show steel ball bouncing higher than rubber ball from same height — steel has higher e

common_misconceptions.belief: After a collision with e < 1, momentum is also lost
common_misconceptions.correction: Momentum is ALWAYS conserved in collisions (when no external force acts), regardless of e. Only kinetic energy is lost when e < 1. The lost KE converts to heat, sound, and deformation — not to momentum.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show momentum bars unchanged before and after collision for all values of e, while KE bars change

legend.meaning: Colliding body dropped from height h
Kinetic energy before and after impact
